To 
Sarah, Ted, Torn S. 
Date 
Oc t. 10, 197 9 
From 
: 
PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Subject: 
Letter from David Nawi, Air Resources Board (ARB) 
All of you should have in your possession a copy of 
Mr. Nawi's memorandum dated October 3, 1979, requesting 
advice regarding Mr. James G. Leather's participation in 
proceedings to amend ARB's agricultural burning regulations. 
Set forth below is additional information obtained from 
Mr. Nawi and Mr. Leathers that was not provided in the ori-
ginal package of materials. 
1. 
Economic impact of the proposed regulations on 
Mr. Leathers' farming interests. 
Mr. Nawi indicated that the staff report on the proposed 
regulations states that farmers, who conduct burnings on 
their acreage, may have additional costs of $.30 per acre. 
Mr. Leathers uses 220 of his 680 acres for rice production. 
The costs to Mr. Leathers of burning the 220 acres would be 
approximately $66.00 if the regulations were implemented. 
If all 680 acres were subject to burning, the additional 
costs to Mr. Leathers would be approximately $204.00. 
An additional cost to farmers imposed by the proposed 
regulations is the $5.00 increase in permit fees to burn 
acreage on "no-burn days." 
The proposed regulation would 
also make it more difficult to receive these permits. 
Mr. Leathers does not believe he will be affected because he 
has never sought a permit to burn on a "non-burn" day and 
does not expect to do so in the future. 
2. 
Possible nexus between the Rice Research Board and 
the proposed regulations on agricultural burning. 
The Rice Research Board was established pursuant to a 
state marketing order for the purposes of promoting the rice 
industry. 
Mr. Leathers is Chairman of the Board. 
According 
to Mr. Leathers and Mr. Nawi, the Rice Research Board did 
t 


Sarah, Ted, Tom S. 
October 10, 1~79 
Page Two 
not participate in the drafing of these regulations although 
opportunities were presented By ARB to all interested parties, 
including the Board, to do so. 
3. 
Effect of the proposed regulations on Mr. Leathers' 
rice co-op. 
Mr. Leathers' rice crop is marketed by a rice co-op. 
The proposed regulations will not affect the rice co-op 
because it is not anticipated that farmer rice production 
will be affected if the proposed regulations are implemented. 
Analysis 
Mr. Leathers is not disqualified because his financial 
interests will not be materially affected by the proposed 
regulations. 
The maximum costs of the proposed regulation 
to Mr. Leathers' farming business is approximately $204. 
This effect is well below the standards used by the Commission 
for determining materiality (see 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702(b)(1) ). 
There is no nexus between Mr. Leathers' role as Chairman 
of the Rice Research Board and the decisions regarding rice 
burnings before the ARB. 
The Board did not assist ARB in 
developing the proposed regulations. 
Although the rice 
industry law later has taken position before the legislature 
regarding rice burnings, Mr. Leathers is not aware of any 
official positions that might have been taken by the Board 
during this period. 
Even if a nexus was present or the proposed regulations 
affected the Rice Research Board, Mr. Leathers is not dis-
qualified because the Board is a public agency. 
The Board 
was established pursuant to a state marketing order and 
board members are designated in the Department of Food & 
Agriculture's Conflict of Interest Code. 
The decision will not affect the rice co-op that mar-
kets Mr. Leathers' rice crop. 
The co-op will market whatever 
rice is provided to it by its memberr. 
Mr. Leathers and 
Mr. Nawi do not force any reduction in rice productivity by 
farmers as a result of these regulations. 
If rice production 
is not affected by the regulations, it is not foreseeable 
that the co-op will be affected.

Page Three 
Mr. Nawi needs a response by close of business today or 
tomorrow morning at the latest. 
Interested persons should 
get together with me this afternoon to discuss my conclusions 
and proposed advice that Mr. Leathers may participate in the 
proceedings amending ARB's agricultural burning regulations.

We request your oplnlon regarding a possible,conflict of 
interest of a newly appointed member to the Air Resources Board, James 
Leathers. Mr. Leathers was appointed to the Board September 10, 1979, 
and has just completed his statement of economic interest, a copy of 
which I enclose. 
On October 12, 1979, the Board will consider amendments to its 
agricultural burning regulations. 
These amendments, if adopted, will 
likely have a significant impact on rice growing operations within the 
state. The proposed amendments are set out and explained in the staff 
report, a copy of which is enclosed for your reference. 
In light of the fact that Mr. Leathers is a farmer and has 
substantial acreage devoted to rice growing, the question has arisen as 
to whether or not he may participate in the Board's action on the proposed 
regulatory changes. 
In addition to Mr. Leathers' activities as a rice 
grower, he is the Chairman of the California Rice Research Board, con-
stituted pursuant to a state marketing ord~r for the purpose of investi-
gating and improving the production of rice. 
I request that on the basis of this information and any other 
information you may require, you provide us with a determination as to 
whether or not Mr. Leathers must be disqualified from participation in 
the Board's consideration of the proposed regulatory amendments. 
We 
would like this determination as soon as possible and in any event prior 
to October 12, 1979, if you can complete it in this time frame. 
(We 
also intend to look into this question in the event you are unable to 
respond by October 12.) 
Please feel free to call on us for any further information you 
may require. 
Thank you for your assistance in this matter·

IV. 
A PROPOSAL FOR REVISION OF THE AGRICULTURAL BURNING GUIDELINES 
AND THE METEOROLOGICAL CRITERIA FOR REGULATING AGRICULTURAL 
BURNING 
A. 
PROPOSED REVISIONS TO THE GUIDELINES APPLICABLE TO THE 
SACRAMENTO AND SAN JOAQUIN VALLEY AIR BASINS 
1. Proposal To P.equire Backfiring or Into-the-Wind 
Striplighting for Certain Field Crop Wastes 
a. 
Reason for Proposed Revision 
Specialized burning techniques known as backfiring or into-
the-wind striplighting were adopted for use on rice straw by the 
districts in the Sacramento Valley in 1975. 
This requirement, 
however, does not appear in the Agricultural Burning Guidelines. 
Research has shown that these burning techniques can significantly 
reduce particulate emissions in rice straw burning. 
Research has also shown that emission reductions can be 
achieved by employing backfiring, or into-the-wind striplighting 
for other cereal crops which are harvested in such a way as to 
permit the use of these methods. 
Three additional crop wastes 
have been identified for which these burning techniques could be 
employed. 
They are barley, wheat and oat straw. 
At the present, 
the waste from these crops is commonly burned using headfires. 
A discussion of each of these three burning techniques follows. 
1 Headfire: 
In the presence of wind, a fire 
lighted on the upwind side of a field is called a headfire. 
Wind 
causes the fire to move across the field rapidly, causing incomplete 
combustion and larger amounts of particulate emissions than a 
backfire. 
Figure IV-l illustrates the relationships between wind 
direction and the direction in which the burn advances when the 
headfire technique is employed. 


Sacramento and San Joaquin Valley Air Basins is essential to the 
air pollution control officers of those districts in evaluating 
the impact on air quality before issuing no-burn day permits. 
The 
Air Resources Board (ARB) can and should provide this information 
to the districts. 
4) 
To offset the additional restrictions suggested above, 
there are permissive-burn days which have exceptionally good 
dispersion capabilities which could be termed "premium permissive-
burn days," and that the agricultural community should be made 
aware of these premium days so that a larger portion of the burning 
could be conducted on such days. 
5) 
The Sacramento Valley Air Basin has adopted rice straw 
burning regulations which have effected emission reductions from 
the traditional methods of drying and burning rice straw. 
The 
adoption and use of these regulations in the San Joaquin Valley 
Air Basin will effect an emission reduction. 
The rice burning 
regulations contained in the Sacramento Valley Air 8asin Plan 
should be applied in the San Joaquin Valley Air Basin and these 
regulations should be adopted into the Agricultural Burning 
Guidelines. 
6) 
On occasion, agricultural burn decisions made at an 
earlier time have not turned out as anticipated and in the case of 
a burn decision a smoke episode was imminent or in the case of a 
no-burn decision a good opportunity to burn was missed. 
In both 
cases the effectiveness of the control program is lessened. 
A 
provision to change the decision after it has been disseminated 
would minimize these situations. 
7) 
In spi te of the oppos iti on expressed by Pl acer County 


groups, the staff believes that inclusion of western Placer 
County in the Sacramento Valley Southeast Section for purposes of 
permissive-burn declarations is necessary. 
This would cause areas 
of similar terrain, crop patterns. and meteorological conditions 
to have uniform permissive-burn declarations. This would improve 
air quality and alleviate the complaints of residents of northeast 
Sacramento County which result from agricultural burning in western 
Placer County under unfavorable meteorological conditions. 
8) 
Sizeable land areas above the floor of the Sacramento 
Valley can have good dispersion of pollutants during burning of 
waste because they are frequently located above the inversion 
layer. Therefore, these areas should have separate burn-day 
declarations made for them. 
9) 
A change in the boundary between the North and South 
Sections of the San Francisco Bay Area Air Basin, as requested by 
the Bay Area Air Quality Management District, would be beneficial. 
The proposed change would resolve enforcement and identification 
problems that have been caused by the present delineation. 
10) 
The application of meteorological criteria for the 
Southeast Desert Air Basin to the desert portion of the San Diego 
Air Basin is appropriate. 
Since the meteorological criteria for 
the San Diego Air Basin reflect conditions in the coastal area of 
the Basin and since dispersion of pollutants at altitudes above 
3,000 feet may provide excellent burn conditions, it is reasonable 
to divide the San Diego Air Basin into two parts, the division to 
be set at 3,000 feet mean sea level, adjustable downward as appropriate. 
This recommendation and the experimentation that it would facilitate 
would be beneficial in that it may result in the prevention of 
! 


catastrophic wildfires, while not adversely affecting the air 
quality, basinwide, in the San Diego Air Basin. 
11) 
Since the San Diego APeD has requested authorization to 
make its own weather forecasts as a precursor to determining 
whether a day should be a burn or no-burn day, and since the 
District has adequate technical staff to operate a forecasting 
program, the ARB staff has no technical objection to letting the 
District do so. 
12) 
The experimental criteria used to determine agricultural-
burn days in the Lake Tahoe Basin have worked well to identify 
permissive-burn days. 
The use of these criteria should be formalized 
and continued. 
13) 
To meet the requirement that 50 percent of the land be 
brush treated to qua 1 ify for range improvement burning on no-burn 
days during January 1 through r'1ay 31, the live:dead ratio of brush 
to be burned should be considered in determining how much of the 
brush has been treated. 
When burning for wildlife and game 
habitat improvement, mechanical or chemical treatment of the brush 
may not be desirable. 
In addition, the Department of Fish and 
Game should have the authority to designate the amount of brush 
treatment when the burn is done primarily for the imDrovement of 
land for wildlife and game habitat. 
14) 
Section B01BO of the Agricultural Burning Guidelines 
pertains to allowing the burning of cotton gin waste as agricultural 
waste until January 1, 1979. 
Since this date has passed and in 
the two years since this section was included in the Guidelines, 
the ARB staff has not been aware of any permits being issued for 
the burning of cotton gin waste, the regulations allowing cotton 


gin waste burning should be deleted. 
15) 
The adoption of the amendments to the Agricultural 
Burning Guidelines proposed by the staff would result in a total 
emissions reduction of about 2,800 tons per year particulate 
matter, 500 tons per year hydrocarbons, and 3,300 tons per year 
carbon monoxide in the Sacramento Valley Air Basin and 2,500 tons 
per year particulate matter, 600 tons per year hydrocarbons, and 
3,800 tons per year carbon monoxide in the San Joaquin Valley Air 
Basin.

I NTRODUCTI ON 
7f-23-2 
September 12, 1979 
October 12, 1979 
Because of the importance of maintaining a viable agricultural 
industry in California and the importance of protecting the 
people of the State of California from the adverse effects of air 
pollution, the Legislature has directed the Air Resources Board 
(ARB) to reasonably regulate, but not prohibit, burning of agricultural 
waste. 
To this end, the ARB, in 1971, after statewide public 
hearings, promulgated its Agricultural Burning Guidelines for six 
California air basins. These Guidelines were subsequently incorporated 
into Title 17 of the California Administrative Code. 
In 1971 the ARB also adopted meteorological criteria for 
determining "permissive-burn days," i.e. days during which meteorological 
conditions would assure adequate dispersion of the pollution 
caused by such burning. 
These criteria are delineated in the 
document entitled Meteorological Criteria for Regulating Agricultural 
Burning. 
On June 21,1972, the Board expanded the Guidelines to 
include regulations on forest management burning, range improvement 
burning, and farm waste burning for all air basins in California. 
Meteorological criteria for regulating agricultural burning were 
also revised at this time. 
I~plementation plans were developed by 


the districts within each air basin and were approved by the 
Board. 
In December 1976 the Guidelines were amended a second time to 
provide regulations for the implementation of AB 2931, which 
allowed cotton gin waste burning to be included in the definition 
of "agricultural burning" and therefore allowed cotton gin waste 
to be burned as an agricultural crop waste. 
An expiration date of 
January 1,1979 was included in the Guidelines. 
The Guidelines were last amended in April 1978 to change 
Section 80110, Permissive-Burn or No-Burn Days, to allow the ARB 
to make burn/no-burn day announcements at 3:00 p.m. daily for 
each following day for each of the air basins in three possible 
ways: 
The announcement would indicate whether the following day 
would be a permissive-burn day, a no-burn day, or the decision 
would be announced the following day at 7:45 a.m. 
Meteorological 
criteria for regulating agricultural burning were also revised at 
this time. 
Currently. the definition of aqricu1tura1 burning included in 
the Guidelines separates agricultural burning into four categories. 
Section 80150 discusses agricultural operations in the growing of 
crops or raising of fowl or animals; Section 80160 discusses range 
improvement burning; Section 80170 discusses forest management 
burning; and Section 80180 discusses the burning of waste from 
cotton ginning operations. 
Since the adoption of the April 1978 revisions of the Guidelines 
and meteorological criteria, several factors have influenced the 
ARB staff to now go before the Board with new changes to the 
Guidelines. 


First, the ARB has continued 
receive complaints 
the 
public which indicate much dissatisfaction with the pollutants 
emitted from the burning of agricultural wastes. 
As shovm in 
Figures 1-1 through I-6 agricultural burning is a significant 
source of air pollution in the Sacramento and San Joaquin ValleY 
Air Basins. 
Second, in 1978 the EPA issued a notice of proposed rule-
making regarding its proposed disapproval 
the provisions of the 
State Implementation Plan which allow special permits for burning 
on no-burn days. 
The EPA's contention is that the special permits 
are based solely on economic factors and contain no provision for 
the achievement and maintenance of ambient air quality standards. 
Third, the California legislature, through the mechanism of

It is important that the meteorological criteria be changed

Therefore, 
based mostly on discussions at recent workshops on agricultural 
burning in the Sacramento Valley and San Joaquin Valley Air Basins, 
several changes in the meteorological criteria are proposed to 
provide optimum effectiveness to the program. 
These changes would 
provide better use of the more favorable burning situations, more 
emphasis on considering ambient air quality exceedances during no-
burn situations, and more flexibility in decision-making by allowing 
decisions, once disseminated, to be changed if the situation 
warrants it. These proposed changes will be discussed separately 
below.

CONCLUSIONS 
1) 
Significant reductions in particulate matter emissions 
can be effected in the Sacramento Valley and San Joaquin Valley 
Air Basins by the employment of backfiring or into-the-wind striplighting 
techniques for the burning of wastes from certain cereal crops. 
The crops which most appropriately lend themselves to these techniques 
are barley, oats. rice and wheat. 
The local air pollution control districts (APCDs) need the 
flexibility to allow exemptions from the requirement to use one of 
these techniques in certain circumstances. 
These circumstances 
would include appropriate consideration for fire hazard. visibility 
obstruction. or cases of insufficient fuel to sustain a backfire. 
The districts should also be provided the flexibility to extend 
the applicability of this requirement to the waste from other 
crops which may demonstrate an emissions reduction if burned by 
backfiring or into-the-wind striplighting. 
2) 
A significant reduction in emissions from agricultural 
burning can be achieved if the burning of field crop wastes in the 
Sacramento Valley and San Joaquin Valley Air Basins does not begin 
until 10:00 a.m. 
3) 
The Agricultural Burning Guidelines do not contain any 
guidance for the issuance of no-burn day permits other than the 
consideration of "imminent and substantial economic loss." The 
Guidelines should be modified to better protect the public health 
by adding ambient air quality criteria which limits burning on 
"no-burn" days to those days when the health-based air quality 
standards would not otherwise be exceeded.

Mr. Leathers is not disqualified because his financial 
interests will not be materially affected by the proposed 
regulations. 
The maximum costs of the proposed regulation 
to Mr. Leathers' farming business is approximately $204. 
This effect is well below the standards used by the Commission 
for determining materiality (see 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702(b)(1) ). 
There is no nexus between Mr. Leathers' role as Chairman 
of the Rice Research Board and the decisions regarding rice 
burnings before the ARB. 
The Board did not assist ARB in 
developing the proposed regulations. 
Although the rice 
industry law later has taken position before the legislature 
regarding rice burnings, Mr. Leathers is not aware of any 
official positions that might have been taken by the Board 
during this period. 
Even if a nexus was present or the proposed regulations 
affected the Rice Research Board, Mr. Leathers is not dis-
qualified because the Board is a public agency. 
The Board 
was established pursuant to a state marketing order and 
board members are designated in the Department of Food & 
Agriculture's Conflict of Interest Code. 
The decision will not affect the rice co-op that mar-
kets Mr. Leathers' rice crop.

rice is provided to it by its memberr. 
Mr. Leathers and 
Mr. Nawi do not force any reduction in rice productivity by 
farmers as a result of these regulations. 
If rice production 
is not affected by the regulations, it is not foreseeable 
that the co-op will be affected.

The facts as I understand them were as follows. 
The Air Resources Board is considering amending its statewide 
agricultural burning regulations. 
These amendments will affect 
the procedures used by farmers who find it necessary to b:.Jrn off 
their fields and will limit the time available to them for burning. 
The Air Resources Board staff report indicates that the economic 
impact of these proposed regulations on growers would be between 
$.15 and $.75 an acre. 
The estimated total economic impact on the 
farming community is expected to be approximately $83,400. 
The 
amendments contemplated by the Air Resources BOcHa are pre:sently 
in effect for most rice growers in the Sacramento Valley at the 
local level because of local air pollution control laws. 
Mr. James A. Leathers is a member ot the Aii Resources Soard. 
Mr. Leathers owns a 680 acre farm in the Sacramento Valley on 
which he grows rice and wheat. 
Two hundred twenty of the 680 
acres farmed by Mr. Leathers would be affected by the proposed Air 
Resources Board burning regulations. 
Mr. Leathers is also a member 
of the rice co-op and the California Rice Research Board, which 
was established pursuant to a state marketing order for the purpose 
of improving the production of. rice.

We request your oplnlon regarding a possible,conflict of 
interest of a newly appointed member to the Air Resources Board, James 
Leathers. Mr. Leathers was appointed to the Board September 10, 1979, 
and has just completed his statement of economic interest, a copy of 
which I enclose. 
On October 12, 1979, the Board will consider amendments to its 
agricultural burning regulations. 
These amendments, if adopted, will 
likely have a significant impact on rice growing operations within the 
state. The proposed amendments are set out and explained in the staff 
report, a copy of which is enclosed for your reference. 
In light of the fact that Mr. Leathers is a farmer and has 
substantial acreage devoted to rice growing, the question has arisen as 
to whether or not he may participate in the Board's action on the proposed 
regulatory changes. 
In addition to Mr. Leathers' activities as a rice 
grower, he is the Chairman of the California Rice Research Board, con-
stituted pursuant to a state marketing ord~r for the purpose of investi-
gating and improving the production of rice. 
I request that on the basis of this information and any other 
information you may require, you provide us with a determination as to 
whether or not Mr. Leathers must be disqualified from participation in 
the Board's consideration of the proposed regulatory amendments. 
We 
would like this determination as soon as possible and in any event prior 
to October 12, 1979, if you can complete it in this time frame. 
(We 
also intend to look into this question in the event you are unable to 
respond by October 12.) 
Please feel free to call on us for any further information you 
may require. 
Thank you for your assistance in this matter· 
D~~' 
David Nawi
sections.question_synthetic: Is James G. Leathers, an Air Resources Board member who owns farmland and has interests in rice-related organizations, disqualified from participating in ARB proceedings to amend agricultural burning regulations that would affect his farming operations?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Mr. Leathers is not disqualified from participating in the ARB proceedings because the proposed regulations would not have a materially significant financial effect on his farming business (estimated impact of only $204) and there is no connection between his Rice Research Board chairmanship and the ARB decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains both an internal memorandum (Oct. 10, 1979) with preliminary analysis and the formal advice letter (Nov. 15, 1979) to David Nawi. Some OCR artifacts present in raw text, particularly in the latter pages containing regulatory text. The formal response begins on the second memo dated Nov. 15, 1979. Key sections clearly identify the question and conclusion regarding materiality of financial interest under Government Code Sections 87100-87103.

QUESTION: Whether Mr. Leathers is disqualified from participating in the hearings because of his farming and related interests in connection with proposed Air Resources Board amendments to agricultural burning regulations.

CONCLUSION: 1) 
Significant reductions in particulate matter emissions 
can be effected in the Sacramento Valley and San Joaquin Valley 
Air Basins by the employment of backfiring or into-the-wind striplighting 
techniques for the burning of wastes from certain cereal crops. 
The crops which most appropriately lend themselves to these techniques 
are barley, oats. rice and wheat. 
The local air pollution control districts (APCDs) need the 
flexibility to allow exemptions from the requirement to use one of 
these techniques in certain circumstances. 
These circumstances 
would include appropriate consideration for fire hazard. visibility 
obstruction. or cases of insufficient fuel to sustain a backfire. 
The districts should also be provided the flexibility to extend 
the applicability of this requirement to the waste from other 
crops which may demonstrate an emissions reduction if burned by 
backfiring or into-the-wind striplighting. 
2) 
A significant reduction in emissions from agricultural 
burning can be achieved if the burning of field crop wastes in the 
Sacramento Valley and San Joaquin Valley Air Basins does not begin 
until 10:00 a.m. 
3) 
The Agricultural Burning Guidelines do not contain any 
guidance for the issuance of no-burn day permits other than the 
consideration of "imminent and substantial economic loss." The 
Guidelines should be modified to better protect the public health 
by adding ambient air quality criteria which limits burning on 
"no-burn" days to those days when the health-based air quality 
standards would not otherwise be exceeded.

of air quality standards on no-burn days in the districts in the

should be reviewed, primarily with their impact on agricultural 
waste burning in the central valleys of California, in order to 
determine where they could be strengthened so that air quality 
there could be improved.

1978 and culminated in the staff preparing a proposal for revising 
the existing Guidelines. 
In addition, the staff scheduled and 
held a series of workshops for the agricultural community at which

Date Oc t. 10, 197 9 From : PRACTICES COMMISSION Subject: Letter from David Nawi, Air Resources Board (ARB) All of you should have in your possession a copy of Mr. Nawi's memorandum dated October 3, 1979, requesting advice regarding Mr. James G. Leather's participation in proceedings to amend ARB's agricultural burning regulations. Set forth below is additional information obtained from Mr. Nawi and Mr. Leathers that was not provided in the ori- ginal package of materials. 1. Economic impact of the proposed regulations on Mr. Leathers' farming interests. Mr. Nawi indicated that the staff report on the proposed regulations states that farmers, who conduct burnings on their acreage, may have additional costs of $.30 per acre. Mr. Leathers uses 220 of his 680 acres for rice production. The costs to Mr. Leathers of burning the 220 acres would be approximately $66.00 if the regulations were implemented. If all 680 acres were subject to burning, the additional costs to Mr. Leathers would be approximately $204.00. An additional cost to farmers imposed by the proposed regulations is the $5.00 increase in permit fees to burn acreage on "no-burn days." The proposed regulation would also make it more difficult to receive these permits. Mr. Leathers does not believe he will be affected because he has never sought a permit to burn on a "non-burn" day and does not expect to do so in the future. 2. Possible nexus between the Rice Research Board and the proposed regulations on agricultural burning. The Rice Research Board was established pursuant to a state marketing order for the purposes of promoting the rice industry. Mr. Leathers is Chairman of the Board. According to Mr. Leathers and Mr. Nawi, the Rice Research Board did t Sarah, Ted, Tom S. October 10, 1~79 Page Two not participate in the drafing of these regulations although opportunities were presented By ARB to all interested parties, including the Board, to do so. 3. Effect of the proposed regulations on Mr. Leathers' rice co-op. Mr. Leathers' rice crop is marketed by a rice co-op. The proposed regulations will not affect the rice co-op because it is not anticipated that farmer rice production will be affected if the proposed regulations are implemented. Analysis Mr. Leathers is not disqualified because his financial interests will not be materially affected by the proposed regulations. The maximum costs of the proposed regulation to Mr. Leathers' farming business is approximately $204. This effect is well below the standards used by the Commission for determining materiality (see 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1) ). There is no nexus between Mr. Leathers' role as Chairman of the Rice Research Board and the decisions regarding rice burnings before the ARB. The Board did not assist ARB in developing the proposed regulations. Although the rice industry law later has taken position before the legislature regarding rice burnings, Mr. Leathers is not aware of any official positions that might have been taken by the
embedding.summary: This advice letter responds to an inquiry about whether Air Resources Board member James G. Leathers has a disqualifying conflict of interest in participating in proceedings to amend agricultural burning regulations. The FPPC concludes he is not disqualified because the financial impact on his farming operations is immaterial and there is no meaningful nexus to his other interests.
